part.text:
The user wants me to:
1. Make the CI target support policy declarative
2. Generate the checked-in workflow from manifests
3. Enforce it stays in sync
4. Preserve current CI behavior unless clearly duplicated policy
5. Keep normal contributor workflow simple
6. Test the generator
7. Commit the finished change

This is a substantial task involving understanding the current CI workflow, extracting target support policy, creating a manifest-based generator, and setting up enforcement. Let me start by exploring the repository structure to understand what we're working with.

I should read the AGENTS.md and relevant docs first, then examine the CI workflows and any existing manifest files.

Implement manifest files and generator

Commit finished change

# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

display.text:
# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

[monorepo]
config_roots = [".devcontainer", "bindings/*", "examples/*", "docs"]

[tools]
# General tooling
"aqua:jdx/usage" = "3.3.0" # CLI completion for mise tasks
"aqua:jdx/hk" = "1.45.0" # Linter orchestration
"aqua:apple/pkl" = "0.31.0" # hk config dependency
"aqua:dprint/dprint" = "0.53.2" # Formatter orchestration
"github:sargunv/jvl" = "2026.05.03" # JSON validator
"aqua:rhysd/actionlint" = "1.7.8" # GH Actions linter
"aqua:koalaman/shellcheck" = "0.11.0" # Shell script linter (used by actionlint)

# C/C++ ecosystem
"github:prefix-dev/pixi" = "0.68.1"
"aqua:Kitware/CMake" = "4.3.3"
"aqua:ninja-build/ninja" = "1.13.2"
"conda:clang-format" = "22.1.7"
"conda:clang-tools" = "22.1.7"
"conda:doxygen" = "1.13.2"
"conda:glslang" = "16.3.0"
"conda:pkg-config" = "0.29.2"
"conda:vulkan-tools" = "1.4.341.0"
"github:cmakefmt/cmakefmt" = "1.3.0"
"aqua:XcodesOrg/xcodes" = { version = "1.6.2", os = ["macos"] }

# Node.js ecosystem
"core:node" = "24.11.0"
"aqua:pnpm/pnpm" = "10.33.2"

# Java/Kotlin ecosystem
"core:java" = "temurin-25.0.3+9.0.LTS"
"aqua:facebook/ktfmt" = "0.62"
"github:google/google-java-format" = "1.35.0"

# Python ecosystem
"core:python" = "3.14.4"
"aqua:astral-sh/uv" = "0.11.11"

# Swift ecosystem
"core:swift" = { version = "6.3.1", os = ["macos"] }
"github:nicklockwood/SwiftFormat" = "0.61.1"

# Other ecosystems
"core:rust" = { version = "1.95.0", profile = "minimal", components = "rustfmt,clippy" }
"core:zig" = "0.16.0"
"core:go" = "1.26.3"
"core:dotnet" = "10.0.203"

[env]
UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"
MLN_FFI_VARIANT = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE = "{{env.MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE | default(value=\"RelWithDebInfo\")}}"
MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/include"
MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/third_party/maplibre-native/vendor/Vulkan-Headers/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"
MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT = "{{env.MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT | default(value=\"30s\")}}"
CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default"
PKG_CONFIG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib/pkgconfig"
LIBCLANG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"

[hooks]
postinstall = [
  { task = "sync-submodules" },
  "hk install --mise --quiet",
  "pixi install --locked --quiet",
  # conda-forge ships libclang-13.dll, but clang-sys on Windows searches for libclang.dll.
  # https://github.com/KyleMayes/clang-sys/issues/213
  # https://github.com/conda-forge/llvmdev-feedstock/issues/376
  '{% if os() == "windows" %}libclang_bin="$(dirname "$MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR")/bin"; cp "$libclang_bin/libclang-13.dll" "$libclang_bin/libclang.dll"{% endif %}',
  "uv sync --locked --quiet",
  "pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --loglevel warn",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest bindings/dotnet/dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  # Astro type stubs for vp typeCheck on docs sources (.astro/ is gitignored).
  "pnpm --filter docs exec astro sync",
]

[tasks.configure]
depends = ["//:deps"]
run = [
  '''
  cmake_args=(
    -S .
    -B "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
    -G Ninja
    -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE="$MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE"
    -DMLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND="$MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND"
    -DMLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER="$MLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER"
    -DMLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT="$MLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT"
  )

  while IFS= read -r cmake_arg; do
    [[ -n "$cmake_arg" ]] && cmake_args+=("$cmake_arg")
  done <<< "${MLN_FFI_CMAKE_ARGS:-}"

  cmake "${cmake_args[@]}"
  ''',
  '''
  if [[ -f "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" ]]; then
    mkdir -p build/clangd
    compile_commands_link="build/clangd/compile_commands.json"
    compile_commands_tmp="${compile_commands_link}.tmp"
    rm -f "$compile_commands_tmp"
    ln -s "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" "$compile_commands_tmp"
    mv -f "$compile_commands_tmp" "$compile_commands_link"
  fi
  ''',
]

[tasks.build]
depends = ["//:configure"]
run = '''
build_args=(
  --build "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
  --parallel
)

cmake "${build_args[@]}"'''

[tasks.clean]
run = "rm -rf \"$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR\""

[tasks.deps]
description = "Install variant-specific managed dependencies."
run = '''
if [[ "$MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE" == "macos-arm64" ]]; then
  mise run deps:angle
fi
'''

[tasks.test]
depends = ["//:build"]
run = '''
(
  cd src/c_api/tests/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
(
  cd bindings/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
'''

[tasks.check]
run = "hk check --all"

[tasks.fix]
run = "hk fix --all"

[tasks.actions-up]
description = "Update and pin GitHub Actions steps."
run = "pnpm dlx actions-up"

[tasks._ktfmt]
description = "Called by dprint.jsonc"
hide = true
run = 'java -jar {{ tools["aqua:facebook/ktfmt"].path }}/ktfmt'
# ktfmt on windows doesn't work in git bash for some reason
shell = '{% if os() == "windows" %}cmd /c{% else %}sh -c -o errexit{% endif %}'

[tasks."ci:lint"]
run = [
  # Warm process-plugin cache before hk runs parallel dprint batches.
  "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
  "hk fix --all",
  "git update-index -q --refresh",
  # Lockfile drift is common across different mise builds
  "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
  "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
]

[tasks."ci:vulkan-info"]
description = "Print the active Vulkan loader and driver summary."
run = "vulkaninfo --summary"

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene

    timeout-minutes: 30

display.text:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run ci:lint

  docs:
    name: docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run //docs:build

  variants:
    name: variant / ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 120
    env:
      MISE_ENV: ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-x64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: linux-x64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-vulkan
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-egl
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-simulator-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: windows-x64-vulkan
            runner: windows-2022
          - mise_env: windows-x64-wgl
            runner: windows-2022
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps

      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run test

      - name: Build Java FFM binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build
      - name: Build Java JNI binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-jni:build
      - name: Test .NET binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/dotnet:test
      # linux-arm64: no Kotlin/Native host — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-36871
      # windows: no MSVC target — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/SKIKO-446
      - name: Test Kotlin/Native binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'windows-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'linux-arm64-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/kotlin-native:build
      - name: Test Rust binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/rust:ci
      - name: Test Swift binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'macos-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/swift:test
      - name: Test Zig binding
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/zig:ci

      - name: Build LWJGL example
        if: ${{ contains(matrix.mise_env, 'vulkan') }}
        run: mise run //examples/lwjgl-map:build
      - name: Test Rust example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/rust-map:ci
      - name: Build Swift example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env == 'macos-arm64-metal' }}
        run: mise run //examples/swift-map:build
      - name: Build Zig example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-map:build
      - name: Run Zig readback example
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-readback:run

  required:
    name: ci-required
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 5
    if: ${{ always() }}
    needs:
      - hygiene
      - docs
      - variants
    steps:
      - name: Fail if required jobs failed
        if: ${{ contains(needs.*.result, 'failure') || contains(needs.*.result, 'cancelled') || contains(needs.*.result, 'skipped') }}
        run: |
          echo '${{ toJSON(needs) }}'
          exit 1

      - run: true

<content>
1: name: Setup CI deps
2: description: Install sccache and mise toolchain
3: 
4: runs:
5:   using: composite
6:   steps:
7:     - name: Enable Git long paths
8:       if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
9:       shell: pwsh
10:       run: git config --global core.longpaths true

display.text:
name: Setup CI deps
description: Install sccache and mise toolchain

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - name: Enable Git long paths
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: git config --global core.longpaths true

    - name: Cache MapLibre Native source
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          third_party/maplibre-native
          .git/modules/third_party/maplibre-native
        key: mln-src-v2-${{ hashFiles('.gitmodules', 'cmake/mln_version.cmake') }}

    - name: Install sccache
      uses: mozilla-actions/sccache-action@9e7fa8a12102821edf02ca5dbea1acd0f89a2696 # v0.0.10

    - name: Use Linux software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        sudo apt-get update
        sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes \
          libegl-mesa0 \
          libgl1-mesa-dri \
          libvulkan1 \
          mesa-utils \
          mesa-utils-extra \
          mesa-vulkan-drivers
        {
          echo "EGL_PLATFORM=surfaceless"
          echo "LIBGL_ALWAYS_SOFTWARE=true"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Use Windows software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $mesaVersion = "26.0.8"
        $mesaArchive = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d.7z"
        $mesaRoot = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d"
        $mesaUrl = "https://github.com/pal1000/mesa-dist-win/releases/download/$mesaVersion/mesa3d-$mesaVersion-release-msvc.7z"

        Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $mesaUrl -OutFile $mesaArchive
        $expectedHash = "A438C26C2752726916E455F9AD121F8A7E3CFECF8626251ABF8E5B3E129D8497"
        $actualHash = (Get-FileHash $mesaArchive -Algorithm SHA256).Hash
        if ($actualHash -ne $expectedHash) {
          throw "Mesa archive hash mismatch: expected $expectedHash, got $actualHash"
        }
        7z x $mesaArchive "-o$mesaRoot" -y | Out-Null

        $mesaWglDriver = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "libgallium_wgl.dll" |
          Where-Object { $_.FullName -match "x64" } |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $mesaWglDriver) {
          throw "Missing Mesa WGL driver in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $driverManifest = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "lvp_icd.x86_64.json" |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $driverManifest) {
          throw "Missing lavapipe Vulkan ICD manifest in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $mesaBin = $mesaWglDriver.Directory.FullName
        $mesaWglSystemDriver = Join-Path $env:WINDIR "System32\mesadrv.dll"
        Copy-Item $mesaWglDriver.FullName $mesaWglSystemDriver -Force

        $openglDriverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\OpenGLDrivers\MSOGL"
        New-Item -Path $openglDriverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DLL -PropertyType String -Value "mesadrv.dll" -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DriverVersion -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Flags -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Version -PropertyType DWord -Value 2 -Force | Out-Null

        $driverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Khronos\Vulkan\Drivers"
        New-Item -Path $driverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $driverKey -Name $driverManifest.FullName -PropertyType DWord -Value 0 -Force | Out-Null

        "GALLIUM_DRIVER=llvmpipe" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV
        "Path=$mesaBin;$env:Path" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Cache Cargo packages
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.cargo/registry/index/
          ~/.cargo/registry/cache/
          ~/.cargo/git/db/
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-cargo-registry-v1-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}

    - name: Cache Gradle
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.gradle/caches
          ~/.gradle/wrapper
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle*', '**/gradle-wrapper.properties') }}
        restore-keys: |
          ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-

    - name: Trim Windows PATH before mise activation
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $workspaceDrive = Split-Path -Qualifier $env:GITHUB_WORKSPACE
        # Git Bash maps /tmp to the checkout drive; create it before mise hooks run.
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Join-Path $workspaceDrive "tmp") | Out-Null

        $paths = @(
          if ($env:SCCACHE_PATH) { Split-Path -Parent $env:SCCACHE_PATH }
          if ($env:Path) { $env:Path -split ';' }
          "C:\Program Files\Git\bin",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32",
          "$env:SystemRoot",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\Wbem",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0",
          "C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\cmd"
        ) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) }

        "Path=$($paths -join ';')" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

        $gradleTemp = $env:RUNNER_TEMP -replace '\\', '/'
        $gradleOptions = "-Djava.io.tmpdir=$gradleTemp"
        if ($env:GRADLE_OPTS) {
          $gradleOptions = "$gradleOptions $env:GRADLE_OPTS"
        }
        "GRADLE_OPTS=$gradleOptions" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Install mise toolchain
      uses: jdx/mise-action@1648a7812b9aeae629881980618f079932869151 # v4.0.1
      with:
        cache: true
        cache_key_prefix: mise-v2
        install: true

    - name: Wire sccache into CMake
      shell: bash
      run: |
        {
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_ENABLED=true"
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_VERSION=${MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE}"
          echo "SCCACHE_BASEDIRS=${GITHUB_WORKSPACE}"
          echo "CMAKE_C_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
          echo "CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Diagnose EGL setup
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        mise exec -- bash -euo pipefail -c '
          env | sort | grep -E "^(EGL|LIBGL|MESA|LD_LIBRARY_PATH|__EGL|MLN_FFI|VK_)" || true
          command -v eglinfo
          EGL_LOG_LEVEL=debug LIBGL_DEBUG=verbose LD_DEBUG=libs eglinfo || true
        '

    - name: Diagnose Vulkan setup
      if: ${{ contains(env.MISE_ENV, 'vulkan') }}
      shell: bash
      run: mise run ci:vulkan-info

    # The mise action may restore Rust metadata without all rustup-managed
    # component files. Repair the toolchain only when rustup cannot add the
    # configured components or the component binaries still cannot run.
    - name: Ensure Rust components
      shell: bash
      run: |
        ensure_rust_components() {
          mise exec -- rustup component add rustfmt clippy &&
            mise exec -- cargo fmt --version &&
            mise exec -- cargo clippy --version
        }

        if ! ensure_rust_components; then
          mise install --force rust
          ensure_rust_components
        fi

<path>/app/.mise/config.linux-x64-vulkan.toml</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: [env]
2: MLN_FFI_VARIANT = "linux-x64-vulkan"
3: MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = "linux-x64"
4: MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = "vulkan"
5: MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = "{{config_root}}/build/linux-x64-vulkan"
6: MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = "x86_64-linux-gnu"
7: MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = "-Dtarget=x86_64-linux-gnu -Dcmake-artifact-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}\" -Drender-backend=vulkan -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Ddependency-library-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}\""
8: BINDGEN_EXTRA_CLANG_ARGS = "{% if os() == \"linux\" %}-isystem{{ exec(command='cc -print-file-name=include', cache_key='host-cc-builtin-include', cache_duration='1d') | trim }}{% endif %}"
9: CMAKE_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
10: LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
11: LD_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
12: RUSTFLAGS = "-C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath-link,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}"

MLN_FFI_VARIANT = "linux-x64-egl"
MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = "linux-x64"
MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = "opengl"
MLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER = "egl"
MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = "{{config_root}}/build/linux-x64-egl"
MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = "x86_64-linux-gnu"
MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = "-Dtarget=x86_64-linux-gnu -Dcmake-artifact-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}\" -Drender-backend=opengl -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Ddependency-library-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}\""
BINDGEN_EXTRA_CLANG_ARGS = "{% if os() == \"linux\" %}-isystem{{ exec(command='cc -print-file-name=include', cache_key='host-cc-builtin-include', cache_duration='1d') | trim }}{% endif %}"
CMAKE_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
LD_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
RUSTFLAGS = "-C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath-link,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}"

1: [env]
2: MLN_FFI_VARIANT = "macos-arm64-metal"
3: MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = "macos-arm64"
4: MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = "metal"
5: MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = "{{config_root}}/build/macos-arm64-metal"
6: MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = "aarch64-macos"
7: MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = "-Dtarget=aarch64-macos -Dcmake-artifact-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}\" -Drender-backend=metal -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Ddependency-library-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}\""
8: MLN_FFI_SYSTEM_ROOT = "{% if os() == \"macos\" %}{% if env.SDKROOT is defined and env.SDKROOT != \"\" %}{{ env.SDKROOT }}{% else %}{{ exec(command='xcrun --sdk macosx --show-sdk-path', cache_key='macosx-sdk-path', cache_duration='1d') | trim }}{% endif %}{% endif %}"
9: JDK_JAVA_OPTIONS = "-Djava.library.path={{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -Dorg.lwjgl.vulkan.libname=libvulkan.1.dylib"
10: VK_ICD_FILENAMES = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/share/vulkan/icd.d/MoltenVK_icd.json"
11: RUSTFLAGS = "-C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}"

(End of file - total 11 lines)
</content>

MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = "-Dtarget=x86_64-windows-msvc -Dcmake-artifact-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}\" -Drender-backend=opengl -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Ddependency-library-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}\""
CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/Library"
CMAKE_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
PKG_CONFIG_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}/pkgconfig"
LIBCLANG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/Library/bin"
RUSTFLAGS = "-L native={{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"

1: ---
2: title: Concepts
3: description: Core mental models for using MapLibre Native FFI.
4: ---
5: 
6: ## Mental Model
7: 
8: MapLibre Native FFI exposes MapLibre Native concepts directly. Applications can
9: use it directly or through language bindings, and higher-level adapters can
10: build on the same model. It provides a common portable API surface for native
11: map integration.
12: 
13: Three concepts form the core API: the runtime, the map, and the render session.
14: Events and bindings connect those concepts to host code.
15: 
16: ## Runtime
17: 
18: The runtime owns scheduler state and event storage for one host owner thread.
19: Host code creates a runtime on the thread that will pump it. Runtime work and
20: events flow through that owner thread.
21: 
22: Each owner thread may have one live runtime. The host pumps that runtime to let
23: MapLibre Native make progress and to collect completed work.
24: 
25: ## Map
26: 
27: A map belongs to a runtime. It owns map state: style documents, sources, layers,
28: images, camera state, feature state, observer events, and render invalidation.
29: 
30: A map is independent of any particular render target. Host code can create,
31: configure, query, and observe the map without tying that map state to a window,
32: surface, or texture.
33: 
34: ## Render Session
35: 
36: A render session renders one map to one render target. Render targets are
37: surfaces or textures.
38: 
39: Surface sessions render and present through caller-provided native surfaces.
40: Texture sessions render offscreen into session-owned backend targets or
41: caller-owned borrowed backend targets.
42: 
43: A map may have one live render session at a time. Keeping render sessions
44: separate from maps lets host code manage graphics backend lifecycle outside the
45: map object itself.
46: 
47: ## Events
48: 
49: Events preserve MapLibre Native's observer-driven model across the FFI boundary.
50: The runtime copies events into host-visible storage, and host code drains those
51: events from the runtime.
52: 
53: Events report map lifecycle, rendering progress, resource activity, diagnostics,
54: and asynchronous failures.
55: 
56: ## Language Bindings
57: 
58: Language bindings preserve the same runtime, map, render session, and event
59: model in the target language. They keep the API portable while matching the
60: target language's handle and error conventions.
61: 
62: Bindings sit directly above the C API and stay close to its shape. They expose
63: the same core objects and relationships with language-appropriate safety around
64: handles, lifetimes, errors, and event draining.

(End of file - total 64 lines)
</content>

display.text:
---
title: Concepts
description: Core mental models for using MapLibre Native FFI.
---

## Mental Model

MapLibre Native FFI exposes MapLibre Native concepts directly. Applications can
use it directly or through language bindings, and higher-level adapters can
build on the same model. It provides a common portable API surface for native
map integration.

Three concepts form the core API: the runtime, the map, and the render session.
Events and bindings connect those concepts to host code.

## Runtime

The runtime owns scheduler state and event storage for one host owner thread.
Host code creates a runtime on the thread that will pump it. Runtime work and
events flow through that owner thread.

Each owner thread may have one live runtime. The host pumps that runtime to let
MapLibre Native make progress and to collect completed work.

## Map

A map belongs to a runtime. It owns map state: style documents, sources, layers,
images, camera state, feature state, observer events, and render invalidation.

A map is independent of any particular render target. Host code can create,
configure, query, and observe the map without tying that map state to a window,
surface, or texture.

## Render Session

A render session renders one map to one render target. Render targets are
surfaces or textures.

Surface sessions render and present through caller-provided native surfaces.
Texture sessions render offscreen into session-owned backend targets or
caller-owned borrowed backend targets.

A map may have one live render session at a time. Keeping render sessions
separate from maps lets host code manage graphics backend lifecycle outside the
map object itself.

## Events

Events preserve MapLibre Native's observer-driven model across the FFI boundary.
The runtime copies events into host-visible storage, and host code drains those
events from the runtime.

Events report map lifecycle, rendering progress, resource activity, diagnostics,
and asynchronous failures.

## Language Bindings

Language bindings preserve the same runtime, map, render session, and event
model in the target language. They keep the API portable while matching the
target language's handle and error conventions.

Bindings sit directly above the C API and stay close to its shape. They expose
the same core objects and relationships with language-appropriate safety around
handles, lifetimes, errors, and event draining.

1: ---
2: title: Overview
3: description: Contributor setup, project scope, workflow commands, tests, and examples.
4: sidebar:
5:   order: 1
6: ---
7: 
8: ## Project Scope
9: 
10: The project exposes MapLibre Native through two layers.
11: 
12: The C API exposes core MapLibre Native features on supported native platforms:
13: runtime, resources, maps, cameras, events, diagnostics, logging, render target
14: primitives, texture readback, and low-level extension points such as resource
15: providers and URL transforms. It excludes convenience APIs such as snapshotting
16: and platform integrations such as gestures and device sensors.
17: 
18: Language bindings sit directly above the C API. In the target language, they
19: manage C handles, struct initialization, scoped lifetimes, status codes,
20: diagnostics, borrowed data, threading, and event draining. They preserve the C
21: API's concepts. Higher-level adapters may provide full SDKs, async models, view
22: lifecycle integrations, convenience workflows, or new abstractions.
23: 
24: Read the
25: [Binding specification](/maplibre-native-ffi/development/binding-specification/)
26: before implementing or reviewing a binding.
27: 
28: ## Getting Set Up
29: 
30: Install the platform toolchain:
31: 
32: - On macOS, install the Xcode version listed in `.xcode-version`, or a recent
33:   compatible Xcode. Run the pinned
34:   [`xcodes`](https://github.com/XcodesOrg/xcodes) tool with `xcodes select` to
35:   switch to the repository version.
36: - On Fedora, install the `C Development Tools and Libraries` package group with
37:   `sudo dnf group install c-development`.
38: - On Debian or Ubuntu, install the C and C++ compiler packages with
39:   `sudo apt install build-essential`.
40: - On Windows, install a recent version of Visual Studio Community (or Build
41:   Tools) 2022 with the `Desktop development with C++` workload and
42:   `Git for Windows`. We rely on Git Bash to run project scripts.
43: 
44: Install [`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/), then install pinned project tools and
45: run repository setup hooks:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: mise install
49: ```
50: 
51: The setup hooks install project dependencies and initialize the MapLibre Native
52: submodule at `third_party/maplibre-native`.
53: 
54: Run the Zig map example as a smoke test:
55: 
56: ```bash
57: mise run //examples/zig-map:run
58: ```
59: 
60: Mise selects the native build profile that matches your host when one is
61: available. Set `MISE_ENV=<variant>` before `mise run ...`, or pass
62: `mise -E <variant> run ...`, to build, test, or run examples for another
63: platform and render backend.
64: 
65: ## Common Commands
66: 
67: ```bash
68: # Build and test the C API (via Zig bindings)
69: mise run test
70: 
71: # Build only
72: mise run build
73: 
74: # Run linters and formatters
75: mise run fix
76: 
77: # Run examples
78: mise run //examples/zig-map:run
79: 
80: # Build the documentation site
81: mise run //docs:build
82: ```
83: 
84: ## How Tools Fit Together
85: 
86: This repository spans native code, language bindings, examples, tests, and
87: documentation. Each tool owns the layer where it has the clearest dependency
88: model.
89: 
90: Project-managed dependency state keeps builds reproducible. Mise supplies
91: repository tools, owns task execution, and exports the environment consumed by
92: build systems, bindings, and examples. Pixi currently supplies desktop native
93: libraries from [`conda-forge`](https://conda-forge.org/). Platform SDKs such as
94: Xcode, Visual Studio, Linux compiler packages, and the Android SDK remain host
95: toolchain inputs.
96: 
97: [`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/) is the contributor entrypoint. It pins top-level
98: tools, installs Git hooks, selects backend variants, and runs repository tasks.
99: Use `mise run ...` for common workflows: build, test, check, fix, and examples.
100: Mise also provides ecosystem entrypoints such as Pixi, Zig, Python, uv, Node,
101: and pnpm.
102: 
103: [`pixi`](https://pixi.sh/) installs the desktop native libraries used by local
104: builds. CMake, Ninja, pkg-config, shader tools, Doxygen, clang-format, and
105: clang-tidy are pinned by mise. Host platform toolchains provide C and C++
106: compilers, and CMake builds the native C/C++ library.
107: 
108: Language package managers own dependencies inside their ecosystems. For example,
109: `uv` owns Python package dependencies, `pnpm` owns Node package dependencies,
110: Gradle owns Java and Kotlin dependencies, and Cargo owns Rust dependencies.
111: Language-specific formatters, linters, analyzers, test frameworks, and code
112: generators usually live with the language package graph they serve.
113: 
114: [`hk`](https://github.com/jdx/hk) orchestrates repository checks for pre-commit,
115: `mise run check`, and `mise run fix`. [`dprint`](https://dprint.dev/) owns
116: repository-wide formatting defaults.
117: 
118: [Astro](https://astro.build/) and [Starlight](https://starlight.astro.build/)
119: build the documentation site. Generated API reference HTML is installed into
120: `docs/public/reference/` before each docs build.
121: 
122: ## Tests And Examples
123: 
124: Every feature needs automated CI coverage when practical. The root
125: `mise run test` command builds the native library, runs the direct Zig C API
126: suite, and runs the public Zig binding suite for supported host variants.
127: 
128: Use examples for demos and behavior that needs manual validation, such as visual
129: output, interactive input, or host graphics integration.
130: 
131: Keep examples small. This repository includes low-level language bindings and
132: focused integration examples. Full application SDKs live outside this
133: repository.

(End of file - total 133 lines)
</content>

display.text:
---
title: Overview
description: Contributor setup, project scope, workflow commands, tests, and examples.
sidebar:
  order: 1
---

## Project Scope

The project exposes MapLibre Native through two layers.

The C API exposes core MapLibre Native features on supported native platforms:
runtime, resources, maps, cameras, events, diagnostics, logging, render target
primitives, texture readback, and low-level extension points such as resource
providers and URL transforms. It excludes convenience APIs such as snapshotting
and platform integrations such as gestures and device sensors.

Language bindings sit directly above the C API. In the target language, they
manage C handles, struct initialization, scoped lifetimes, status codes,
diagnostics, borrowed data, threading, and event draining. They preserve the C
API's concepts. Higher-level adapters may provide full SDKs, async models, view
lifecycle integrations, convenience workflows, or new abstractions.

Read the
[Binding specification](/maplibre-native-ffi/development/binding-specification/)
before implementing or reviewing a binding.

## Getting Set Up

Install the platform toolchain:

- On macOS, install the Xcode version listed in `.xcode-version`, or a recent
  compatible Xcode. Run the pinned
  [`xcodes`](https://github.com/XcodesOrg/xcodes) tool with `xcodes select` to
  switch to the repository version.
- On Fedora, install the `C Development Tools and Libraries` package group with
  `sudo dnf group install c-development`.
- On Debian or Ubuntu, install the C and C++ compiler packages with
  `sudo apt install build-essential`.
- On Windows, install a recent version of Visual Studio Community (or Build
  Tools) 2022 with the `Desktop development with C++` workload and
  `Git for Windows`. We rely on Git Bash to run project scripts.

Install [`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/), then install pinned project tools and
run repository setup hooks:

```bash
mise install
```

The setup hooks install project dependencies and initialize the MapLibre Native
submodule at `third_party/maplibre-native`.

Run the Zig map example as a smoke test:

```bash
mise run //examples/zig-map:run
```

Mise selects the native build profile that matches your host when one is
available. Set `MISE_ENV=<variant>` before `mise run ...`, or pass
`mise -E <variant> run ...`, to build, test, or run examples for another
platform and render backend.

## Common Commands

```bash
# Build and test the C API (via Zig bindings)
mise run test

# Build only
mise run build

# Run linters and formatters
mise run fix

# Run examples
mise run //examples/zig-map:run

# Build the documentation site
mise run //docs:build
```

## How Tools Fit Together

This repository spans native code, language bindings, examples, tests, and
documentation. Each tool owns the layer where it has the clearest dependency
model.

Project-managed dependency state keeps builds reproducible. Mise supplies
repository tools, owns task execution, and exports the environment consumed by
build systems, bindings, and examples. Pixi currently supplies desktop native
libraries from [`conda-forge`](https://conda-forge.org/). Platform SDKs such as
Xcode, Visual Studio, Linux compiler packages, and the Android SDK remain host
toolchain inputs.

[`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/) is the contributor entrypoint. It pins top-level
tools, installs Git hooks, selects backend variants, and runs repository tasks.
Use `mise run ...` for common workflows: build, test, check, fix, and examples.
Mise also provides ecosystem entrypoints such as Pixi, Zig, Python, uv, Node,
and pnpm.

[`pixi`](https://pixi.sh/) installs the desktop native libraries used by local
builds. CMake, Ninja, pkg-config, shader tools, Doxygen, clang-format, and
clang-tidy are pinned by mise. Host platform toolchains provide C and C++
compilers, and CMake builds the native C/C++ library.

Language package managers own dependencies inside their ecosystems. For example,
`uv` owns Python package dependencies, `pnpm` owns Node package dependencies,
Gradle owns Java and Kotlin dependencies, and Cargo owns Rust dependencies.
Language-specific formatters, linters, analyzers, test frameworks, and code
generators usually live with the language package graph they serve.

[`hk`](https://github.com/jdx/hk) orchestrates repository checks for pre-commit,
`mise run check`, and `mise run fix`. [`dprint`](https://dprint.dev/) owns
repository-wide formatting defaults.

[Astro](https://astro.build/) and [Starlight](https://starlight.astro.build/)
build the documentation site. Generated API reference HTML is installed into
`docs/public/reference/` before each docs build.

## Tests And Examples

Every feature needs automated CI coverage when practical. The root
`mise run test` command builds the native library, runs the direct Zig C API
suite, and runs the public Zig binding suite for supported host variants.

Use examples for demos and behavior that needs manual validation, such as visual
output, interactive input, or host graphics integration.

Keep examples small. This repository includes low-level language bindings and
focused integration examples. Full application SDKs live outside this
repository.

state.output:
<path>/app/docs/src/content/docs/development/binding-specification.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: ---
2: title: Binding specification
3: description: Specification for low-level language bindings over the C API.
4: sidebar:
5:   order: 3
6: ---
7: 
8: Specification for language binding subprojects that expose MapLibre Native
9: through the public C API.
10: 
11: ## Scope
12: 
13: Every binding gives host code a safe, low-level way to use MapLibre Native
14: through `maplibre_native_c.h`.
15: 
16: A binding exposes MapLibre concepts directly, keeps native ownership and
17: borrowed data safe in the target language, reports native failures through the
18: target language's error model, and tests the supported C API domains through the
19: public binding.
20: 
21: The required binding layer is a low-level FFI API. It MUST NOT add
22: application-framework policy, UI/view lifecycle integration, general async APIs,
23: or scheduler models above the C API concepts.
24: 
25: A subproject that needs host-thread confinement uses the owner-thread helper
26: design in Threading. No other execution model belongs in the binding layer.
27: 
28: ---
29: 
30: ## Priorities
31: 
32: When requirements conflict, resolve them in this order:
33: 
34: 1. Target-language safety idioms over cross-binding alignment.
35: 2. Cross-binding alignment over target-language syntactic idioms.
36: 
37: Cross-binding alignment means bindings expose the same C API concepts, ownership
38: rules, operation boundaries, and error semantics. It does not require identical
39: syntax, names, or package structure.
40: 
41: Bindings are minimal and complete:
42: 
43: - Minimal: public APIs map to C API concepts and binding-owned safety policy.
44:   They expose one correct low-level public API for each C API operation and do
45:   not add redundant workflows, shortcuts, higher-level composites, or alternate
46:   ownership, threading, callback, allocation, or error contracts.
47: - Complete: a binding that claims support for a C API domain exposes that
48:   domain's public operations, input shapes, event payloads, status behavior, and
49:   ownership rules through public wrapper types. Unsupported domains stay absent
50:   from the safe public API.
51: 
52: ## Architecture
53: 
54: Every binding splits implementation into three layers:
55: 
56: | Layer                  | Responsibility                                                                                       |
57: | ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
58: | Raw C layer            | Generated or tool-imported declarations for the public C headers, raw structs, constants, and calls. |
59: | Internal support layer | Status conversion, diagnostics, handle state, memory guards, callback state, native loading, shims.  |
60: | Public binding layer   | Handles, input types, values, events, callbacks, errors, and render-target APIs for users.           |
61: 
62: Requirements:
63: 
64: - Raw C declarations MUST stay outside the safe public API. If the target
65:   language cannot hide raw declarations, they MUST live under a generated
66:   interop namespace and be excluded from the supported safe API surface.
67: - Host FFI carrier types and raw entrypoints MUST stay outside the safe public
68:   API. Public APIs expose backend addresses only through `NativePointer`.
69: - Public examples and tests MUST use the public binding layer, not raw C calls,
70:   except bindability and layout tests.
71: - Raw C declarations MUST be generated or tool-imported from public headers.
72:   Generated files MUST be mechanically reproducible from public headers,
73:   metadata, or generator inputs checked into the repository.
74: - Handwritten support code MUST follow repeatable patterns that can be audited
75:   across domains.
76: - Native library loading and ABI-version checks MUST fail with a binding error
77:   before a public wrapper stores an incompatible native handle. The expected ABI
78:   version MUST come from generated binding metadata or a checked-in binding
79:   constant, and ABI mismatch MUST use a stable public error category.
80: 
81: ---
82: 
83: ## Naming
84: 
85: This specification uses generic concept names. Bindings apply target-language
86: naming and packaging conventions while preserving the concept and ownership
87: semantics.
88: 
89: Use `MapLibre` when referring to the project or upstream library in prose.
90: Inside code identifiers, treat `maplibre` as one word and apply the target
91: language's normal casing rules:
92: 
93: ```text
94: MaplibreMap
95: maplibre_map
96: maplibreMap
97: MAPLIBRE_MAP
98: ```
99: 
100: Do not split the brand into separate identifier words such as `MapLibreMap` or
101: `map_libre_map`.
102: 
103: Long-lived C-owned opaque handle concepts include:
104: 
105: - `RuntimeHandle`
106: - `MapHandle`
107: - `MapProjectionHandle`
108: - `RenderSessionHandle`
109: - `OfflineOperationHandle`
110: - `ResourceRequestHandle`
111: 
112: `Handle` means the public value owns or controls an explicitly releasable native
113: resource with identity across operations. The representation can vary by
114: binding; the ownership contract does not.
115: 
116: ---
117: 
118: ## Handle Lifetime
119: 
120: Public handles are for values that own or control native state across calls,
121: such as runtimes, maps, render sessions, offline operations, resource requests,
122: and acquired texture frames. Input values, events, diagnostics, query results,
123: snapshots, and native-filled structs become copied language values. Native
124: snapshot, result, and list handles remain internal implementation details.
125: 
126: ### Owned handles
127: 
128: Every public wrapper that owns or controls native state across calls MUST store:
129: 
130: - private native identity;
131: - live/releasing/closed state;
132: - the native destroy function or bridge release path;
133: - parent references or lifetime evidence required for native validity;
134: - callback state owned by that handle's native scope;
135: - leak-reporting context when the binding has non-deterministic cleanup hooks.
136: 
137: Native identity MUST NOT be public. The implementation can represent it with a
138: native pointer, bridge-owned handle, or private table ID, but every public
139: operation uses the same ownership rules.
140: 
141: Public release follows this operation:
142: 
143: 1. If the wrapper is already closed, return success without calling native code.
144: 2. If another release is in progress, wait for it or return the binding's
145:    in-progress error before calling native code.
146: 3. Mark the wrapper as releasing so public methods fail before calling native
147:    code.
148: 4. Keep owner-scoped support state live, including parent references, callback
149:    state, and request registries.
150: 5. Invoke the matching native release path.
151: 6. If native release succeeds, mark the wrapper closed and make later release
152:    calls no-op.
153: 7. If native release fails, restore the live state and return the native error
154:    with diagnostics. Consuming or move-based release APIs return the live owner
155:    state so callers can retry.
156: 
157: Deterministic cleanup hooks follow the same release operation when they can
158: report release failure through the target language's normal error path.
159: Non-deterministic cleanup hooks report leaks for thread-affine handles. They
160: MUST NOT destroy runtime, map, projection, or render-session handles from
161: cleanup hooks. Infallible language destructors that attempt best-effort release
162: MUST preserve the explicit release contract and MUST NOT mask native errors from
163: the explicit release path.
164: 
165: ### Parent validity
166: 
167: Bindings MUST preserve native parent validity while child wrappers are live:
168: 
169: Child wrappers retain the parent owner state whenever native validity depends on
170: the parent. Releasing a parent while children are live MUST fail without
171: consuming or destroying the parent.
172: 
173: `MapProjectionHandle` is the exception: after creation it owns a standalone
174: projection snapshot. It MUST remain valid after the source map closes and MUST
175: release with `mln_map_projection_destroy()`.
176: 
177: ### Handle copying
178: 
179: Owned handle wrappers are affine: the safe public API MUST NOT create duplicate
180: owners. Reference-copy languages MUST make all references share one owner state.
181: Value-copy languages MUST make owned handles non-copyable or move-only. Public
182: code MUST NOT be able to fabricate live handles, ID-backed operation tokens, or
183: request tokens from raw integers, raw addresses, public fields, or ordinary
184: constructors.
185: 
186: ---
187: 
188: ## Status And Diagnostics
189: 
190: Every status-returning C call maps to the target language's normal error
191: mechanism.
192: 
193: Status handling follows this operation:
194: 
195: 1. Run binding-owned validation before calling C when the binding can detect the
196:    failure itself.
197: 2. Report binding-owned validation through the same public error family as
198:    native failures, with a fresh binding diagnostic and no stale thread-local C
199:    diagnostic.
200: 3. When a C call returns non-OK, copy `mln_thread_last_error_message()` on the
201:    same thread before any later diagnostic-writing C call.
202: 4. Convert each known non-OK status to the corresponding documented public error
203:    kind. The error kind is stable API and is separate from the diagnostic
204:    string.
205: 5. Convert unknown future status values to the documented unknown-status error
206:    kind and carry the raw native value.
207: 6. Expose the copied diagnostic through the public error. When the target
208:    language's error mechanism cannot carry payloads, as with Zig error tags,
209:    expose a diagnostic store for the failing call and document how child handles
210:    inherit it.
211: 
212: Bindings validate binding-owned state before calling C. They do not duplicate
213: MapLibre or native validation that the C API already performs.
214: 
215: ---
216: 
217: ## Type Mapping
218: 
219: Bindings translate C data shapes into public language values without exposing
220: ABI bookkeeping.
221: 
222: ### Input Structs and Values
223: 
224: C option structs map to language-owned public types, such as classes, records,
225: or structs.
226: 
227: Public callers set semantic fields. ABI `size` fields, masks, and raw nested C
228: storage stay inside C struct materializers.
229: 
230: C struct materialization follows this operation:
231: 
232: 1. Call the C default initializer for each defaultable C struct before setting
233:    public fields.
234: 2. Set semantic fields from the public value.
235: 3. Encode optional fields with explicit present/absent state so present zero
236:    values remain distinguishable from absent values.
237: 4. Keep native input storage alive for the full C borrow window, including
238:    nested input trees and interior pointers.
239: 
240: Structs that C fills and returns by value become copied language values.
241: 
242: ### Enums and masks
243: 
244: Public enum values convert to C through a complete named-case mapping. Public
245: values represent valid C enum inputs.
246: 
247: When C returns an enum value that the binding does not know yet, the binding
248: preserves the raw value instead of collapsing it to a known case. Public input
249: paths reject values outside the C input contract before crossing into C.
250: 
251: Public bit masks use a named public type that supports combining, testing, and
252: empty values. C field masks stay internal to C struct materializers.
253: 
254: ### Strings
255: 
256: Public string inputs use UTF-8 at the C boundary. Null-terminated `const char*`
257: inputs reject embedded `NUL`. `mln_string_view` inputs pass UTF-8 bytes and byte
258: length, and allow embedded `NUL` when the C contract allows it. Borrowed C
259: strings and string views are copied before their native borrow window ends.
260: 
261: ### JSON and GeoJSON
262: 
263: Structured JSON and GeoJSON models MUST preserve MapLibre value semantics:
264: object member order, repeated member names, signed and unsigned integer width,
265: floating-point values, booleans, nulls, strings, arrays, and nested objects. Raw
266: JSON or GeoJSON text inputs MUST pass through as text without reparsing or
267: reformatting unless the public API is explicitly a structured-value API.
268: 
269: Bindings MUST preserve the full unsigned 64-bit domain for unsigned JSON and
270: GeoJSON integer values. Languages without a native unsigned 64-bit integer type
271: MUST expose those values through a documented representation that preserves the
272: raw value. The binding documentation MUST state how callers compare and format
273: representations that are not naturally unsigned.
274: 
275: ### Native pointers
276: 
277: `NativePointer` represents a borrowed opaque backend-native address.
278: 
279: `NativePointer` transfers no ownership and grants no general memory access.
280: Public APIs accept it only where the C API accepts host-owned opaque backend
281: handles. Conversion from raw addresses or raw pointers is internal or exposed as
282: an unsafe or borrowed backend-interop constructor that states the backend
283: lifetime and synchronization requirements.
284: 
285: Backend pointers returned from acquired texture frames perform active-frame
286: checks before exposing the pointer.
287: 
288: ---
289: 
290: ## Data Ownership
291: 
292: Bindings keep C borrow windows explicit and expose stable language-owned values.
293: 
294: ### Temporary native storage
295: 
296: Bindings materialize most native input at the call boundary.
297: 
298: Native input materialization follows this operation:
299: 
300: 1. Allocate or borrow temporary native input storage.
301: 2. Initialize out parameters and callback response structs to the C API's
302:    neutral value before user code runs.
303: 3. Keep temporary storage alive until the C call returns or the full documented
304:    native borrow window ends.
305: 4. Release temporary native allocations on every failure path.
306: 
307: Public objects store temporary pointers only when the object owns that native
308: storage and releases it deterministically.
309: 
310: ### Output values
311: 
312: Bindings expose C outputs as language-owned values unless the result owns or
313: controls native state across calls.
314: 
315: Native snapshot, result, and list handles are internal implementation details.
316: Plain value outputs with no interior borrowed pointers are copied by value.
317: 
318: Outputs backed by native storage follow this operation:
319: 
320: 1. Acquire the native snapshot, result, list, or event.
321: 2. Copy public data into language-owned values before the native borrow window
322:    ends.
323: 3. Preserve unknown event and payload domains as raw values with copied payload
324:    bytes when the C API exposes those bytes.
325: 4. Release native snapshot, result, and list handles exactly once after copying,
326:    including failure paths.
327: 
328: Runtime event polling returns values independent of the next native poll.
329: Map-originated events identify a live source map when identity can be proven. If
330: lookup misses, they carry no public map handle or only copied source metadata.
331: 
332: ## Callbacks And Requests
333: 
334: Callbacks and request handles preserve C lifetimes while protecting
335: host-language state.
336: 
337: ### Callback lifetime
338: 
339: Callback state is retained by the native scope that can invoke it and remains
340: live until replacement, clearing, owner release, native unregistration, and
341: in-flight invocations can no longer reach it.
342: 
343: Callback invocation follows this operation:
344: 
345: 1. Copy callback arguments into language-owned values before user code receives
346:    them. Lexical views are allowed only when the public type prevents retention
347:    beyond the invocation.
348: 2. Host-language failures must not unwind or otherwise escape across the C
349:    callback boundary. If the public callback returns a recoverable host failure,
350:    convert the failure to the C callback's documented behavior.
351: 3. Synchronize callback state that native can invoke concurrently.
352: 4. Return promptly. Callback code hands owner-thread work back to the owner
353:    thread before calling runtime or map APIs.
354: 
355: Callback replacement installs the new native registration before releasing old
356: callback state. If installation fails, the old callback remains active and the
357: replacement state is released. Clearing, replacing, or closing prevents new
358: upcalls, waits for in-flight upcalls, and releases callback roots after native
359: can no longer invoke them.
360: 
361: If a leaked native owner can still reach callback user data, non-deterministic
362: cleanup reports the leak and keeps callback memory reachable from native alive.
363: Style-scoped callback retention follows current native source ownership, not
364: stale event timing or source ID reuse alone.
365: 
366: ### Resource transforms
367: 
368: Resource transform callbacks are synchronous.
369: 
370: Resource transform invocation follows this operation:
371: 
372: 1. Copy request URL and metadata into language-owned values before user code
373:    receives them.
374: 2. Initialize the native response shape to pass-through.
375: 3. If user code returns a replacement URL, copy it into the native response
376:    shape before the callback returns.
377: 4. If user code returns no rewrite or fails validation, keep pass-through
378:    behavior.
379: 5. Host-language failures must not unwind or otherwise escape across the C
380:    callback boundary. If the public handler returns a recoverable host failure,
381:    convert the failure to the C callback's documented behavior.
382: 
383: ### Resource providers
384: 
385: Resource providers decide whether a request passes through to the native
386: provider or is handled by the binding.
387: 
388: Resource provider invocation follows this operation:
389: 
390: 1. For pass-through requests, return pass-through without retaining the native
391:    request handle.
392: 2. For handled requests, copy request fields before user code can retain them.
393: 3. Retain the native request reference until completion, cancellation handling,
394:    or release.
395: 4. Treat inline completion during the provider callback as handled ownership,
396:    even if the callback return path would otherwise pass through.
397: 5. Allow deferred or cross-thread completion when the C API allows it, without
398:    changing one-shot or release behavior.
399: 
400: Handled request completion is terminal. A request can complete once; a
401: completion that reaches C consumes the completion path even when native returns
402: non-OK. Release runs once, waits for in-flight completion or cancellation
403: checks, and makes later completion or cancellation checks fail before crossing
404: into C. Stale public request handles cannot affect later native requests.
405: 
406: ---
407: 
408: ## Threading
409: 
410: The C API owner-thread model is visible at the binding layer. Ordinary public
411: methods call C synchronously on the calling native thread and surface the C
412: owner-thread status when called from the wrong thread.
413: 
414: ### Owner-thread helpers
415: 
416: Provide an owner-thread execution helper when the host language can move a
417: logical task across native threads or cannot otherwise give safe callers a
418: stable native owner thread for a runtime/map lifecycle. Bindings with stable
419: native caller identity expose ordinary methods and wrong-thread errors without
420: this helper.
421: 
422: The helper follows this design:
423: 
424: 1. It owns or binds one native owner thread before creating thread-affine
425:    handles.
426: 2. It runs submitted operations by calling the ordinary low-level binding
427:    methods on that owner thread.
428: 3. It serializes submitted operations with event polling and close on that owner
429:    thread.
430: 4. It returns the ordinary binding result or error shape, including copied
431:    native diagnostics.
432: 5. Closing rejects new submissions, releases thread-affine handles on the owner
433:    thread, and leaves later submissions in the binding's closed-state error
434:    shape.
435: 
436: ### Event polling
437: 
438: The public event API is explicit: host code pumps native runtime work, then
439: polls one queued runtime event. Polling returns one copied event or empty.
440: 
441: Event polling follows this operation:
442: 
443: 1. Initialize the native event struct and `has_event` out parameter before
444:    calling C.
445: 2. Call the C poll function on the runtime owner thread.
446: 3. If no event is available, return the language's empty result.
447: 4. Copy the event type, source type, status code, message bytes, payload bytes,
448:    and typed payload fields before another poll can invalidate native event
449:    storage.
450: 5. Decode known typed payloads only after validating their native size. Preserve
451:    unknown event and payload domains with their raw values and copied payload
452:    bytes.
453: 6. Resolve event source identity through binding-owned runtime state. A
454:    map-originated event may reference an existing public map wrapper or copied
455:    map identity only when the binding can prove that identity. It never creates
456:    a public handle from the native source pointer.
457: 7. Apply binding-owned state updates triggered by the event before returning the
458:    copied event.
459: 
460: ### Transferability
461: 
462: When the language can declare or enforce cross-thread transferability, ordinary
463: owner-thread handles MUST be non-transferable. A transferable owner-thread
464: helper handle is allowed only when every operation is submitted back to the
465: bound native owner thread. Copied immutable values can be transferable when
466: their contents are independent of native owner-thread state. Unchecked or unsafe
467: concurrency conformance MUST name the synchronization invariant that makes it
468: sound.
469: 
470: ---
471: 
472: ## Rendering
473: 
474: Rendering bindings expose render sessions, frame lifetimes, and readback without
475: taking ownership of caller-owned backend resources.
476: 
477: ### Render sessions
478: 
479: Render-session attach APIs cover the C API session families:
480: 
481: - Surface sessions render and present through a host surface.
482: - Session-owned texture sessions render into a texture or image created by the
483:   session.
484: - Caller-owned texture sessions render into a host-owned texture or image.
485: 
486: Attach follows this operation:
487: 
488: 1. Materialize the backend-specific public descriptor into the matching C
489:    descriptor.
490: 2. Pass backend-native host resources as `NativePointer` values.
491: 3. Call the matching C attach function on the map owner thread.
492: 4. Return a distinct `RenderSessionHandle` for the map's one live render
493:    session.
494: 5. Surface unsupported backend, unsupported render-target mode,
495:    existing-session, wrong-thread, and native errors through the binding's
496:    status mapping.
497: 
498: For host-owned backend resources, the binding does not release or synchronize
499: those resources. The caller keeps them valid for the C API's documented borrow
500: window.
501: 
502: The public handle exposes:
503: 
504: - `resize` for session kinds that support resize;
505: - `render_update` for the latest available map render update;
506: - `detach`, which keeps the public handle live after backend resources detach;
507: - `close` or `destroy`, using the owned-handle release operation.
508: 
509: ### Texture frames
510: 
511: Session-owned texture frames are scoped borrows.
512: 
513: Frame acquisition follows this operation:
514: 
515: 1. Acquire the native frame and create an explicit frame handle.
516: 2. Copy public metadata from the native frame.
517: 3. Expose backend handles only through active-frame checked accessors.
518: 4. While the frame is active, reject nested frame acquisition and every exposed
519:    session operation whose C contract forbids execution during an active frame.
520: 5. Release follows the owned-handle release operation. Failed native frame
521:    release leaves the frame live for retry.
522: 6. If wrapper construction fails after native frame acquisition, release the
523:    acquired native frame.
524: 
525: Copyable frame handles include stale-handle protection so old frame copies
526: cannot expose backend handles after release or after a later frame reuses the
527: same storage.
528: 
529: ### Readback
530: 
531: CPU texture readback accepts caller-owned mutable storage.
532: 
533: Readback returns copied `TextureImageInfo` metadata. Buffer-capacity failures
534: preserve the caller's buffer ownership and map to the binding's error mechanism.
535: Public buffer reads return copied or read-only views unless the binding proves
536: exclusive mutable access.
537: 
538: ---
539: 
540: ## Test Cases
541: 
542: Each binding test suite includes the tests below. Conditional tests become
543: required when the binding has the named host-language mechanic, configured
544: render backend, or C API platform support.
545: 
546: Public behavior tests use public binding APIs and real C calls when behavior
547: crosses the binding/C boundary. Tests focus on high-value native workflows and
548: binding-owned safety invariants, not trivial constant assertions or exhaustive
549: invalid-input matrices for validation owned by C.
550: 
551: ### Test execution strictness
552: 
553: Missing dependencies, configured render backends, platform setup, or CI
554: capabilities are test failures, not skips. Skips are limited to tests that are
555: inapplicable because the configured backend or platform support is absent, or
556: the target is outside the binding's documented supported platforms.
557: 
558: Skips MUST be declared explicitly in the test file or through a shared
559: capability check. Each skip states the inapplicable backend, platform, or
560: host-language condition. Individual tests MUST NOT convert setup, loading,
561: rendering, or native-call failures to skips.
562: 
563: ### Test seams
564: 
565: Tests SHOULD use public binding APIs for public behavior. Internal test seams
566: are allowed for behavior that cannot be produced reliably through the public
567: native library:
568: 
569: - ABI mismatch before public handle creation;
570: - native status conversion for status categories whose C producers are
571:   nondeterministic, backend-dependent, or native-exception-only;
572: - unknown future status, enum, event, or payload values;
573: - native destroy, request release, frame release, and callback-install failure;
574: - allocation or copy failure after a native snapshot/list/result handle is
575:   acquired;
576: - in-flight or concurrent callback and release races that require deterministic
577:   scheduling.
578: 
579: Internal seams MUST assert the same public error, lifetime, and cleanup behavior
580: that a real native failure would expose.
581:

585: | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
586: | BND-001 | ABI-version mismatch returns the binding's ABI-version error before storing a public native handle, using an internal loader or version seam. |

594: | BND-022 | A native diagnostic is copied immediately and remains available after a later C call changes thread-local state.                          |
595: | BND-023 | Binding-owned closed-handle validation returns the documented public error before crossing into C.                                        |
596: | BND-024 | Invalid string input containing embedded `NUL` is rejected for null-terminated C inputs.                                                  |
597: | BND-025 | Binding-owned validation produces a fresh binding diagnostic and does not expose stale native thread-local diagnostics.                   |

707: | ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
708: | BND-044 | Non-deterministic cleanup hooks report leaked thread-affine handles rather than destroying them.                                 |
709: | BND-048 | Best-effort cleanup failure is reported through the binding's documented leak or failure channel and explicit release can retry. |
710: 
711: #### Cross-thread public handle use
712: 
713: When safe public code can call owner-thread-affine APIs from the wrong native
714: thread or race release on the same owner-thread handle, include:
715: 
716: | ID      | Test                                                                                                     |
717: | ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
718: | BND-046 | Concurrent releases call native release at most once and public calls fail while release is in progress. |
719: | BND-190 | Owner-thread-affine calls from a different native thread report the binding's wrong-thread error.        |
720: | BND-191 | Runtime wrong-thread errors include the copied native diagnostic.                                        |
721: 
722: #### Owner-thread execution adapters
723: 
724: When the subproject ships an owner-thread execution adapter, include:
725: 
726: | ID      | Test                                                                       |
727: | ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
728: | BND-192 | The adapter confines create, pump, event polling, and close to one thread. |

(End of file - total 728 lines)
</content>

through the public C API.

## Scope
